title: From children to parents: The role of performance shifts in private tutoring enrollment by social origins. The South Korean case
abstract: This article examines the role that achievement and social origins play in the use of private tutoring in South Korea. I employ a rational choice approach to analyse how patterns of achievement affect the use of supplementary education by social origins. By applying a dynamic random effects probit model to the Korean Youth Panel Survey, I find that patterns of achievement have more weight in the decision-making process of disadvantaged parents. This suggests that disadvantaged students must consistently perform at a high level in order to substantially boost their chances of enrolling in private tutoring. The results also suggest that private education investment tends to have a positive effect on students' chances of becoming high achievers, although there are substantial differences by social origins.
journal: Research in Social Stratification and Mobility
